description: Large switch statement based on JsonScope and character, requiring modification for new JSON constructs.
reasoning: The `doPeek` method contains a large `switch` statement that dispatches logic based on the current `JsonScope` and the next character read from the input. If new JSON constructs (e.g., new types of literals, comments, or structural elements) were to be supported, this `switch` statement would need to be modified by adding new `case` branches or altering existing logic. This directly violates the Open/Closed Principle, as extending the parsing capabilities requires modifying existing, tested code rather than extending it.

description: Switch statement on internal peeked token type, requiring modification for new token types.
reasoning: The `peek` method uses a `switch` statement on the `peeked` integer constant to return the corresponding `JsonToken`. If new internal token types (e.g., `PEEKED_BINARY`) were introduced to support new JSON features, this `switch` statement would need to be modified. While `JsonToken` is an enum and often implies a closed set, the internal `PEEKED_X` constants are implementation details that, if extended, would force changes here.

description: If-else if chain for different name token types, requiring modification for new name representations.
reasoning: The `nextName` method uses an `if/else if` chain to handle different ways a JSON name can be represented (unquoted, single-quoted, double-quoted). If a new representation for names were introduced (e.g., backtick-quoted names), this method would need to be modified to include a new `else if` branch. This violates OCP as new name formats cannot be added without altering existing code.

description: Switch statement on peeked token type for skipping values, requiring modification for new token types.
reasoning: The `skipValue` method contains a `switch` statement that dispatches logic based on the `peeked` token type. Each `case` handles a specific type of JSON token (begin array, begin object, string, number, etc.). If new JSON token types were introduced, this `switch` statement would need to be modified by adding new `case` branches. This violates OCP because extending the set of recognizable JSON tokens requires altering this method.

description: Hardcoded list of built-in TypeAdapterFactories, requiring modification for new core type adapters.
reasoning: The `createFactories` method explicitly instantiates and adds a long, hardcoded list of built-in `TypeAdapterFactory` implementations (e.g., `TypeAdapters.JSON_ELEMENT_FACTORY`, `CollectionTypeAdapterFactory`, `MapTypeAdapterFactory`). description: Switch statement on current scope, requiring modification for new JSON structural rules.

description: Hardcoded list of Number subclasses, requiring modification for new Number types.

This is a clear OCP violation because extending the set of known valid number types requires modifying existing code.

description: Base class throwing UnsupportedOperationException for primitive accessors, requiring all subclasses to implement or throw.
reasoning: The `getAsBoolean()` method (and other `getAsX()` methods like `getAsNumber()`, `getAsString()`, etc.) in the `JsonElement` base class throws an `UnsupportedOperationException`. This means that any new subclass of `JsonElement` (even if custom subclasses are discouraged) would be forced to either implement all these methods or explicitly override them to throw the same exception. If a new primitive accessor (e.g., `getAsDate()`) were added, all existing `JsonElement` subclasses would need modification. This violates OCP because extending the capabilities of `JsonElement` requires modifying all concrete implementations.

reasoning: The `peek` method contains a long `if/else if` chain that inspects the type of the object on top of the internal stack (`o instanceof Iterator`, `o instanceof JsonObject`, `o instanceof JsonArray`, `o instanceof JsonPrimitive`, `o instanceof JsonNull`).
